4. Outros Elementos
Campos de preenchimento opcional
4.1 Informações sobre a performance da organização, em comparação com benchmarks internos (ex: outras
unidades) ou externos (ex: organizações do mesmo setor).
Este é o primeiro ano em que a WayCarbon divulgará os resultados de seu inventário de emissões de GEE. Foram
elaborados indicadores de desempenho que serão monitorados nos anos seguintes e, assim, permitirão
acompanhamento do desempenho climático da WayCarbon.
Em relação a benchmarks externos, buscamos realizar análises comparativas utilizando como fontes de informação
os inventários mais recentes disponíveis no Registro Público do Programa Brasileiro do GHG Protocol, publicados
pelas sete empresas do mesmo setor da WayCarbon também participantes do Programa até 2012. Não foi possível
realizar análises comparativas de performance, seja pela inexistência de indicadores publicados ou pela
insuficiência de informações sobre a forma de cálculo dos índices divulgados.
4.2 Descrição de indicadores de emissão de GEE para as atividades da organização. Por exemplo, tCO e/produtos
2
fabricados.
a) Emissões totais de Escopo 1, 2, e 3 no ano de referência / Número médio de colaboradores durante o ano de
referência = 1,910058 tCO2e/un.;
b) Emissões totais de Escopo 1, 2, e 3 no ano de referência / Faturamento bruto total no ano de referência =
0,012108 tCO2e/10^3*R$;
c) Emissões totais de Escopo 1, 2, e 3 no ano de referência / Projetos comerciais faturados no ano de referência =
0,585157 tCO2e/un.;
d) Emissões de Escopo 2 no ano de referência / Área média de escritório no ano de referência = 0,004334
tCO2e/(m2*ano);
e) Aquisição de energia elétrica (Escopo 2) (origem da energia: SIN e geração elétrica emergencial) / Área média de
escritório no ano de referência = 0,043307 MWh/(m2*ano).
4.3 Descrição de estratégias e projetos para a gestão de emissões de GEE.
Uma vez que as viagens aéreas são as principais fontes de emissão da empresa, a política interna de viagens
preconiza que, antes de adquirir passagens para viagens aéreas a negócio, os colaboradores avaliem a
possibilidade de realizar reuniões por meio de ferramentas de telecomunicação, tais como como áudio ou
videoconferência, a fim de se evitar o deslocamento e, consequentemente, emissões de GEE. Além disso, a política
incentiva o deslocamento de apenas um colaborador por viagem.
Como estratégia de gestão de GEE, a implementação futura do software de gestão de emissões de GEE (CLIMAS,
desenvolvido pela WayCarbon) em 2014 permitirá melhoria e excelência na gestão interna de emissões, o
estabelecimento e acompanhamento de metas, bem como abertura e monitoramento de planos de ação para a
correção de desvios.
Vale destacar também que a WayCarbon compensou a totalidade de suas emissões averiguadas no ano 2013
03/12/2019 19:21 10
Inventário 2013 - WayCarbon
(escopos 1, 2 e 3).
4.4 Informações sobre contratos com clientes e fornecedores que incluam cláusulas vinculadas à elaboração de
inventários de GEE e/ou ao envio de informações relacionadas.
Não aplicável.
4.5 Informações sobre incertezas, exclusões de fontes de dados e outras características da elaboração do
inventário.
As fontes de GEE foram identificadas, hierarquizadas de acordo com os processos e atividades da WayCarbon e
categorizadas segundo as especificações do Programa Brasileiro do GHG Protocol. Foi estruturado um banco de
dados no qual cada instância representa uma fonte de GEE. Para cada fonte foram registrados uma série de
atributos, entre eles o "grau" dos dados de entrada; Grau primário indica que os dados são medidos ou obtidos
diretamente. Grau secundário indica valores de referência ou dados estimados a partir de dados primários. Grau
terciário indica que os dados foram estimados a partir de dados secundários.
A partir dessa estruturação de informações, foi possível determinar o percentual de emissões calculado a partir dos
diferentes "graus" dos dados de entrada.
Assim sendo, foi possível verificar que 74,57% do total de emissões foram calculados a partir de dados secundários,
23,33% a partir de dados terciários e, finalmente, 2,11% foram calculados utilizando-se dados primários.
A grande concentração de emissões calculadas a partir de dados secundários e terciários (98% do total de
emissões) deve-se à natureza das atividades mais preponderantes para as emissões da WayCarbon (i.e. viagens
aéreas, deslocamento casa-trabalho e táxi, atividades que, juntas, somam 94,5% das emissões apuradas em 2013) e
da forma indireta com que suas emissões são calculadas.
Contudo, vale destacar que 30% das emissões de viagens aéreas (ou seja, 19% das emissões totais) foram
calculadas a partir de dados terciários e os 70% restantes foram calculadas a partir de dados secundários. A
utilização de dados terciários para essa calcular essa fonte de emissão foi necessária, uma vez que, até maio de
2013, as informações de viagens e trechos aéreos não eram sistematizadas de modo compatível com o cálculo de
emissões. A partir do referido mês, as viagens aéreas passaram a ser controladas e registradas por meio do "Travel
Management System - TMS", fornecido pela agência de viagens com a qual a WayCarbon firmou parceria. O TMS
permite a emissão de relatórios a partir dos quais se pode obter as informações necessárias ao cálculo de emissões
(i.e. trechos viajados e passageiros por trecho). Para 2014, esperamos que a utilização de dados terciários no cálculo
das emissões de viagens aéreas seja nulo, reduzindo em cerca de 84% a utilização de dados terciários.
Vale ainda destacar que, sempre quando pertinente, foi aplicado o princípio de conservadorismo para a estimativa
de emissões. Por exemplo, para o cálculo de emissões oriundas da atividade de deslocamento casa-trabalho,
assumiu-se que os colaboradores percorrem esse trajeto em todos os dias úteis de trabalho, desconsiderando,
assim, dias em que estiveram em viagens ou trabalhando em home-office. Outro exemplo de conservadorismo
consiste no cálculo das emissões oriundas da utilização de táxi; para a conversão de reais gastos em distância
percorrida (km), foram sempre consideradas as tarifas mais baixas (i.e. bandeira 1) e desconsiderados os valores de
bandeiradas; assumiu-se que os táxis utilizam o combustível gasolina.
4.6 Descrição sobre ações internas para melhoria da qualidade do inventário de GEE. Por exemplo, sistematização
da coleta de dados, contratação de verificação externa, etc.
03/12/2019 19:21 11
Inventário 2013 - WayCarbon
O "core business" da WayCarbon refere-se à prestação de serviços de consultoria sobre gestão estratégica de
mudanças climáticas. Sendo assim, um dos objetivos estratégicos da empresa consiste em empregar as melhores
práticas de gestão de emissões de GEE em seus processos internos.
Nesse sentido, os sistemas de gestão da WayCarbon estão parametrizados de modo a permitir a agregação que
possibilite o cálculo de emissões de GEE. Ademais, desenvolvemos um software de gestão de emissões de gases de
efeito estufa (CLIMAS), que entrou em etapa comercial em 2014 e será utilizado pela própria WayCarbon em sua
gestão de emissões de GEE a partir de 2014. O uso do software permitirá melhoria na qualidade do inventário por
meio da coleta automatizada de dados, melhor rastreabilidade de informações, utilização de fatores de emissão
atualizados e pré-carregados, estabelecimento e acompanhamento de metas, bem como abertura e
monitoramento de planos de ação.
Outra ação importante consiste na divulgação interna (intranet) e externa (Programa Brasileiro do GHG Protocol)
dos resultados do relativos ao inventário de 2013. Além disso, o inventário 2013 foi verificado por parte
independente, visando aumentar a credibilidade dos resultados perante o público interno e externo.
4.7 Informações sobre a compra de energia elétrica oriunda de fonte renovável.
Não foi relatado.
4.8 Informações sobre autoprodução de energia oriunda de fonte renovável para consumo próprio.
Não foi relatado.

inventariado.
Não foi relatado.

Campos de preenchimento opcional
4.1 Informações sobre a performance da organização, em comparação com benchmarks internos (ex: outras
unidades) ou externos (ex: organizações do mesmo setor).
Este é o segundo ano em que a WayCarbon divulgará os resultados de seu inventário de emissões de GEE. No ano
anterior (2013) foram elaborados indicadores de desempenho que permitem o acompanhamento do desempenho
climático da WayCarbon. A comparação 2013/2014 desses indicadores está mostrada na próxima sessão.
Em relação a benchmarks externos, buscamos realizar análises comparativas utilizando como fontes de
informação os inventários mais recentes disponíveis no Registro Público do Programa Brasileiro do GHG Protocol,
publicados pelas sete empresas do mesmo setor da WayCarbon também participantes do Programa até 2013. Não
foi possível realizar análises comparativas de performance, seja pela inexistência de indicadores publicados ou
pela insuficiência de informações sobre a forma de cálculo dos índices divulgados.
4.2 Descrição de indicadores de emissão de GEE para as atividades da organização. Por exemplo, tCO e/produtos
2
fabricados.
Em relação ao ano 2013, em 2014 apresentou crescimento de 12,85% no faturamento, crescimento de 6,83% no
número médio de colaboradores e aumento de 10,91% no número de projetos desenvolvidos. Contudo, as
emissões totais neste ano diminuíram 5,12%. Portanto, de maneira geral, os índices de desempenho climático da
WayCarbon tanto em termos absolutos quanto relativos, apresentaram melhora, como apresentado a seguir.
Emissões totais / Número médio de colaboradores = 1,696363 tCO2e/un. (@2013 = 1,910058);
b) Emissões totais / Faturamento bruto total = 0,010178 tCO2e/10^3*R$ (@2013 = 0,012108);
c) Emissões totais / Projetos comerciais faturados = 0,500566 tCO2e/un. (@2013 = 0,585157);
d) Emissões de Escopo 2/ Área média de escritório = 0,007933 tCO2e/(m2*ano) (@2013 = 0,004334, houve aumento
nesse indicador devido ao crescimento no número de colaboradores utilizando o mesmo espaço e devido ao
aumento do fator de emissão do SIN);
e) Aquisição de energia elétrica (Escopo 2) (origem da energia: SIN e geração elétrica emergencial) / Área média de
escritório = 0,054904 MWh/(m2*ano) (@2013 = 0,043307).
4.3 Descrição de estratégias e projetos para a gestão de emissões de GEE.
No ano 2014, a WayCarbon desenvolveu e implementou o software CLIMAS (Climate Management System).
Aplicável a organizações de qualquer porte e atuantes em quaisquer setores produtivos, o CLIMAS é uma
ferramenta para a transição para a economia de baixo carbono e para a excelência na gestão dos impactos da
organização sobre as mudanças globais do clima.
As principais funcionalidades do CLIMAS incluem: (a) Cálculo automático de emissões e remoções de gases de efeito
estufa em tempo real a partir da entrada de dados de atividade das operações corporativas, realizada
mensalmente. (b) Os dados de atividade podem ser inseridos manualmente no sistema por meio da tela de Planilha
ou obtidos através interfaces com planilhas Excel ou extração de dados a partir dos outros sistemas utilizados na
empresa (ERP, sistemas legados, etc.); (c) geração de relatórios de resultados anuais de acordo com programas de
reporte como o GHG Protocol, Carbon Disclosure Project (CDP), Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) e outros; (d) Permite
diferentes perfis de acesso e registro de alterações no sistema; (e) Permite a formulação e acompanhamento de
metas de emissões; (f) Permite a formulação e acompanhamento de projetos para tratamento de desvios; (g)
rastreabilidade e transparência total de dados.
Além disso, o CLIMAS possibilita o acompanhamento dos indicadores de desempenho climático em todas as

unidades gerenciais da empresa, bem como possibilita comparações com metas estabelecidas.
4.4 Informações sobre contratos com clientes e fornecedores que incluam cláusulas vinculadas à elaboração de

4.5 Informações sobre incertezas, exclusões de fontes de dados e outras características da elaboração do
inventário.
As fontes de GEE foram identificadas, hierarquizadas de acordo com os processos e atividades da WayCarbon e
categorizadas segundo as especificações do Programa Brasileiro do GHG Protocol. Para cada fonte foram
registrados uma série de atributos, entre eles o "grau" dos dados de entrada; Grau primário indica que os dados
são medidos ou obtidos diretamente. Grau secundário indica valores de referência ou dados estimados a partir de
dados primários. Grau terciário indica que os dados foram estimados a partir de dados secundários.
A partir dessa estruturação de informações, foi possível determinar o percentual de emissões calculado a partir
dos diferentes "graus" dos dados de entrada.
Assim sendo, verificou-se que 85,7% (74,57% em 2013) do total de emissões foram calculados a partir de dados
secundários, e que 14,3% (2,11% em 2013) foram calculados utilizando-se dados primários. Não foram calculadas
emissões a partir de dados terciários (23,33% das emissões de 2013 foram calculadas a partir de dados terciários).
Tais números representam uma considerável melhora nas fontes de dados utilizados nos cálculos do em relação ao
ano anterior. Essa melhora é atribuível principalmente ao fato que 100% das viagens aéreas tiveram seus trechos
devidamente registrados.
4.6 Descrição sobre ações internas para melhoria da qualidade do inventário de GEE. Por exemplo, sistematização
da coleta de dados, contratação de verificação externa, etc.
O core business da WayCarbon refere-se à prestação de serviços de consultoria sobre gestão estratégica de
mudanças climáticas. Sendo assim, um dos objetivos estratégicos da empresa consiste em empregar as melhores
práticas de gestão de emissões de GEE em seus processos internos. Nesse sentido, é nossa meta manter a
qualificação “Ouro” de nosso inventário no Programa Brasileiro do GHG Protocol.
Para tanto, os sistemas de gestão da WayCarbon estão parametrizados de modo a permitir a agregação que
possibilite o cálculo de emissões de GEE. Ademais, desenvolvemos um software de gestão de emissões de gases de
efeito estufa (CLIMAS), que entrou em etapa comercial em 2014 e é utilizado pela própria WayCarbon em sua
gestão de emissões de GEE desde 2014. O uso do software garante a qualidade do inventário por meio de suas
características de rastreabilidade de informações, utilização de fatores de emissão registrados em banco de
dados curado, estabelecimento e acompanhamento de metas
Além da gestão diligente, a WayCarbon compensa anualmente 100% de suas emissões de GEE de escopo 1, 2 e 3.

1 INTRODUCTION
Conestoga Rovers e Associados Engenharia Ltda has commissioned DNV Climate Change
Services AS (DNV) to carry out the verification and certification of emission reductions
reported for the CDM project activity 4211 “Manaus Landfill Gas Project” in Brazil (the
project) for the period 1 February 2013 to 31 December 2013. This report contains the
findings from the verification and a certification statement for the certified emission
reductions.
1.1 Objective
Verification is the periodic independent review and ex post determination by a Designated
Operational Entity (DOE) of the monitored reductions in GHG emissions that have occurred
as a result of the registered CDM project activity during a defined monitoring period.
Certification is the written assurance by a DOE that, during a specific period in time, a project
activity achieved the emission reductions as verified.
The objective of this verification was to verify and certify emission reductions reported for
the “Manaus Landfill Gas Project” for the period 1 February 2013 to 31 December 2013.
1.2 Scope
The scope of the verification is to verify that:
• The project activity has been implemented and operated in accordance with the
registered PDD or any approved revised PDD;
• The monitoring plan complies with the monitoring methodology and the actual
monitoring complies with the monitoring plan, including compliance with any
guidance provided by the Board regarding deviations from the provisions of a
registered plan and/or methodology;
• The data and calculation of GHG emission reductions have been assessed to
correctly support the emission reductions being claimed.
The verification shall ensure that reported emission reductions are complete and accurate in
order to be certified.
1.3 Description of the project activity
Project Parties: Brazil (host Party), United Kingdom of Great Britain and
Northern Ireland (Annex I Party)
Title of project activity: Manaus Landfill Gas Project
UNFCCC registration No: 4211
Baseline and
monitoring methodology ACM0001 (version 11)

DET NORSKE VERITAS
Verification / Certification Report
Manaus Landfill Gas Project
MANAGING RISK
Project Participants: Conestoga Rovers e Associados Engenharia Ltda, Tumpex
Empresa Amazonense de Coleta de Lixo Ltda and Enterpa
Engenharia Ltda from Brazil, and Summit Lake Limited
and Dasco Partners LLP from United Kingdom of Great
Britain and Northern Ireland
Location of the project activity: Brazil, City of Manaus, State of Amazonas at Km 19 of
Highway AM-010
Project’s crediting period: 8 July 2011 to 7 July 2018
Period verified in this verification: 1 February 2013 to 31 December 2013
The “Manaus Landfill Gas Project” was implemented with the objective to capture, flare and
generate electricity with the landfill gas generated at the landfill of Manaus, at Manaus
Municipality, in order to avoid emissions of methane to the atmosphere produced by the
anaerobic decomposition of the dumped waste from Manaus Municipality and to avoid
emissions of CO by the electricity supplied to the local grid.
2
The project involved the construction of a LFG collection system consisting of vertical and
horizontal LFG extraction wells, centrifugal blower(s), and all other supporting mechanical
and electrical subsystems and appurtenances necessary to collect the LFG. The facilities were
supplied by John Zink /36/ (enclosed flaring) and Landtec /37/ (capture and monitoring). The
project started operations on 8 July 2009. The LFG electricity generation component
contained in the revised PDD has not been installed yet. The revised PDD foresees electricity
generation start up in the second year of project operation. Since project registration, project
participant is seeking a potential electricity buyer in order to firm a long term power purchase
agreement and initiate the electricity generation procurement and construction.
1.4 Methodology for determining emission reductions
The emission reductions are calculated using the methane actually destructed in an enclosed
flare. The methane is determined by the direct and continuous measurement of the actual
quantity LFG that was captured, the content of methane present in the LFG and the
combustion efficiency of the enclosed flares. The project emissions are determined by the
amount of electricity, diesel and LPG consumed by the project activity by the LFG capturing
and management system. The ex-ante parameters are the methane density of 0.0007168
tCH /m3CH at 0°C and 1 atm, which is the International Standard temperature and pressure
4 4
/39/, and the GWP of 25 tCO e/tCH established for the period in 2013 /54/.
2 4
2 METHODOLOGY
DNV has assessed and determined that the implementation and operation of the project
activity, and the steps taken to report emission reductions comply with the CDM criteria and
relevant guidance provided by the Board.
The assessment involved a desk review of relevant documentation as well as an on-site
visit(s). These include:
i) The reading values of LFG flow to the flare (Nm3/h) every two minutes (average of

Duration of verification
Monitoring report publication: 13 January 2014
Desk review: 3 February 2014 to 4 February 2014
On-site assessment: 6 February 2014
Reporting, calculation checks and QA/QC: 3 February 2014 to 9 April 2014
2.1 Desk review
In addition to the monitoring report /1/ (version 1 dated 10 January 2014 and version 2 dated
7 April 2014), DNV reviewed:
• The PDD for the project activity (version 4 dated 24 May 2013) /31/;
• The previous verification report /32/;
• Baseline and monitoring methodology ACM0001, version 11 /50/;
• The validation report, SGS Report CDM VAL 0377 2011 Rev 5, 29 June 2009 /34/.
Besides the above mentioned documents, DNV also assessed other additional documents that
were required to assess the accuracy of the emission reduction calculations presented in the
monitoring report /10/ - /15/.
A list of key documents is given in the reference section of this report.
2.2 On-site assessment
On 6 February 2014 DNV performed on-site assessments. During the on-site assessment
DNV confirmed the actual implementation of the project against that mentioned in the PDD
/31/. This included confirming the operational stages of the project with physical and
documented evidence /3/-/7/. Figures quoted in the monitoring report were cross-checked by
reviewing operation records /8//9/. The persons interviewed during the audit are listed in the
reference section of this report /55/-/57/. Moreover, the supporting spreadsheets for
calculations of Manaus Landfill Gas Project CERs were checked during the visit /2/.
During this site visit and according to the PDD /31/, DNV was able to confirm the landfill
area, where the trenches and vertical wells were installed in order to collect the LFG
generated. The landfill is well operated, with waste compaction and daily cover with soil, and
the project’s capture and flaring station has the operation license /17/.
The information provided in the monitoring report was assessed by:
- Verifying the performance Landtec AEMS (Automated Extraction Monitoring
System) records /3/ - /7/, assess the spreadsheet calculation /2/, calibration certificates
/10/ - /15/, and hourly aggregation spreadsheet /8/. These relevant documents are
required for emission reduction calculations to assure that the data provided in the
monitoring report is reliable and that operation and maintenance of the monitoring
equipment is correct.
- Assessing of raw data from Landtec AEMS against the records provided by the project
participant by sampling. Since the record of each parameter is done every two
minutes, one day produces 720 records. Considering the monitoring period from 1
February 2013 to 31 December 2013, the total sample size is 240 480 line records.
Sampling procedure was based on the American Military Standard and tables for
Inspection by Attributes (MIL-STD-105E) /41/ Level II, single sample for normal
inspection. Since data size is higher than 1 200, the sample size should be 100. The

Interviewing of Olga Corona /55/, Andrés Lorenzo /56/ and Lucas Santos da Costa /57/

2.3 Closing out of verification findings
The objective of this phase of the verification was to resolve any issues which needed be
clarified prior to DNV’s conclusion that i) the project activity has been implemented and
operated in accordance with the registered PDD, ii) the monitoring plan complies with the
monitoring methodology and the actual monitoring complies with the monitoring plan and iii)
the data and calculation of GHG emission reductions are correct.
A corrective action request (CAR) is issued, where:
i. Non-conformities with the monitoring plan or methodology are found in monitoring
and reporting and has not been sufficiently documented by the project participants, or
if the evidence provided to prove conformity is insufficient;
ii. Modifications to the implementation, operation and monitoring of the registered
project activity has not been sufficiently documented by the project participants;
iii. Mistakes have been made in applying assumptions, data or calculations of emission
reductions which will impair the estimate of emission reductions;
iv. Issues identified in a FAR during validation to be verified during verification have not
been resolved by the project participants.
A clarification request (CL) shall be raised if information is insufficient or not clear enough to
determine whether the applicable CDM requirements have been met.
A forward action request (FAR) is issued for actions if the monitoring and reporting require
attention and/or adjustment for the next monitoring period.
The verification identified six CARs and two CLs. No FARs were identified. The CARs and
CLs were satisfactorily addressed by the project participants by among other revising the
monitoring (please refer to Appendix A for further details). Besides the changes made to the
monitoring report as a result of the verification findings, no further changes to the monitoring
report (version 2 dated 7 April 2014) were made compared to the initial version of the
monitoring report received for verification (version 1 dated 10 January 2014).

3 VERIFICATION FINDINGS
This section summarises the findings from the verification of the emission reductions reported
for the “Manaus Landfill Gas Project” for the period 1 February 2013 to 31 December 2013.
3.1 Remaining issues, CARs, FARs from previous validation / verification
This is the third verification of the project. There are no remaining issues or FARs from the
previous verification report /32/.
3.2 Post registration changes
There were no post registration changes identified by DNV during this verification.
3.3 Project implementation
As part of the site visit DNV was able to confirm that the project implementation is in
accordance with the project description contained in the PDD (version 4 of 24 May 2013)
/31/.

The LFG energy generation component foreseen in the PDD has not been installed yet, since
the PDD states that the electricity generation start-up will take place only during the second
year of project operations /31/. After the project registration, project participant is seeking a
potential electricity buyer in order to firm a long term power purchase agreement and initiate
the electricity generation procurement and construction.
The landfill received waste in cells and after covering it was possible to install the wells or
trenches and piping to collect the LFG and conduct to the exhaust system.
After pumping, the LFG flow is measured through a unique thermal mass flow meter. In
accordance with the methodology ACM0001 (version 11) /50/, no separate monitoring of
temperature is necessary when using flow meters that automatically measure temperature and
pressure /10/.

The CH content of LFG is continuously sampled in the same section i.e. pipe section before
4
the flare, and measured automatically every 2 minutes /38/. A set of temperature meters have
been installed at 4 places on the flare and control the inlet dampers to control optimum flare
conditions, and ensure that the temperature inside the flare remains above 500ºC /12/.
The CH and O content of the flare exhaust gas are continuously sampled in the flare outlet,
4 2
and measured automatically every 2 minutes /14/.
All monitoring is carried out through Landtec AEMS (Automated Extraction Monitoring
System) /37/ which collects all monitoring and control parameters and automatically sends the
measurement results through internet to Landtec server in USA including any operation
alarms and troubleshooting. The system has local back-up for 24 hours and continuous back-
up at the Landtec server /37/.
3.4 Information (data and variables) provided in the monitoring report
that is different from that stated in the PDD
The emission reductions are based on the figures sourced from the Landtec AEMS records.
During the monitoring period 1 February 2013 to 31 December 2013 information provided in
the monitoring report /1/ was compared to the data and variables stated in the PDD /31/ with
regard to:
- Flow of LFG to the flare: The parameter is estimated ex-ante in the PDD based on the
potential of LFG generation in the landfilled waste and the capture efficiency of the
LFG in the waste mass. In the CER calculation spreadsheet of the PDD /33/, the
estimated average amount for the year 2013 was 121 994 968 Nm3 (or 111 828 720
Nm3 for eleven months). The amount of LFG captured during monitoring period of 1
February 2013 to 31 December 2013 was 43 490 189 Nm3 /2/, i.e., 64.35% less than
estimated. The difference is related to restriction in the LFG capture system. During
the site visit /55/-/57/, DNV could confirm that the capture system in the landfill was
not entirely implemented, and the drainage trenches in the waste mass were under
construction. Hence, there are improvements in the collection system to be done. The
project participant is improving the quality and quantity of wells, trenches and
pipelines in order to reach the expected amount of captured LFG declared in the PDD;
- Methane content in the LFG collected and sent to the flare: In order to estimate the
CERs in the PDD, the methane content in the LFG was admitted as 50%. During the
monitoring period of 1 February 2013 to 31 December 2013, the average methane
content was about 52.33% /2/.
- Temperature of the flare: no differences in the flare temperature were detected. The
flare is subject to regular maintenance in order to maintain favourable burning
conditions. The methane destruction efficiency is directly related to the flare
temperature. In order to estimate the CERs in the PDD /31/, the flare efficiency was
considered 98%. During the monitoring period of 1 February 2013 to 31 December
2013, the flare efficiency remained above 88%. The difference is related to non-
available data for methane concentration in the exhaust gas for some periods. When
data was not available, the flare efficiency was considered as zero which lead to a low
flare efficiency.
- Electricity generated in the plant: The parameter is estimated ex-ante in the PDD
based on the number of engines installed. In the CER calculation spreadsheet of the
PDD /33/, the estimated average amount for the year 2013 was 55 537 MWh (or 50

There is a delay from 19 July 2013 to 9 October
2013.
Therefore, paragraphs 238 to 242 of the “Clean
Development Mechanism Validation and
Verification Standard” /47/ were used to
calculate the discount to be applied in the
emission reductions for this period where the
meters were out of calibration. According to
paragraphs 238 to 242 of the Clean
Development Mechanism Validation and
Verification Standard /47/ project participants
should apply the maximum error between the
maximum permissible error of the instrument
according to the manufacture and the result of
the delayed calibration to all the measured
values of the period which the calibration was
delayed.
Since the error showed in the calibration
certificate /10/ was smaller than the maximum
permissible error of the meter (± 1% full scale),
the maximum permissible error of the meter
was discounted from all landfill gas captured
values from 19 July 2013 to 9 October 2013.

3.7 Assessment of data and calculation of emission reductions
The emission reduction calculations are based on the figures sourced from the Landtec AEMS
historical records for the following parameters:
- Flow of LFG to the flare;
- Methane content in the LFG collected and sent to the flare;
- Temperature of the flare;
- Methane and oxygen content in the flare exhaust gases.
These data are transferred into a master excel spreadsheet used to aggregate the hourly values
of the recorded 2 minutes figures according to the methodology. These hourly numbers are
transferred to monthly spreadsheet calculations which determine the volume of LFG, and the
CO equivalent reduced (tCO e), whilst considering the methane density of 0.0007168
2 2
(tonCH /m3CH ) for (0°C and 1atm) and GWP of 25 as established for the period in 2013
4 4
/54/.
The project emissions from the project activity include the emissions from consumption of
electricity and emissions from the consumption of fossil fuel, calculated using formulae
provided in the Tool to calculate baseline, project and/or leakage emissions from electricity
consumption (version 1) /52/ and Tool to calculate project or leakage CO emissions from
2
fossil fuel combustion (version 2) /53/, respectively.

The emission reductions for the period have been analysed against the estimated CERs in the

3.8 Quality of evidence to determine emission reductions
DNV was able to confirm that the calculations are based on the authentic data from the
Landtec AEMS historical records /3/ - /7/ and also the monthly systematic and periodical
monitoring of the electricity consumption by the project /16/. The spreadsheets used to
aggregate the hourly values of the 2 minutes recording figures and the spreadsheets /8/ used to
calculate the monthly certified emission reductions (CER) calculations /2/ and all figures were
tracked, checked by sampling /9/ and found to be consistent.
The monitoring information is stored on server computer of Landtec site, and the spreadsheets
are stored on server of Conestoga-Rovers Brazil, both with regular and systematic back-up.
3.9 Management system and quality assurance
The monitoring is carried out through the Landtec Field Service automatic unit /38/, which
include beyond several other parameters, the follow as the main:
• LFG flow;
• CH
4
content of LFG, including the calibration with certified standard gas;
• CH
4
and O
2
content in the exhaust gas, including the daily calibration with certified
standard gas, and
• Flare temperature on three levels, which is considered to calculate the “Net Flare
Operational Period”.
These figures are registered every two minutes and stored in Landtec recorder system at
Landtec server, connected through internet with controlled password. In addition, these are
stored as PDF copies on local computer and safety copies on CDR media, stored on the
Manaus Landfill Gas Project sites.
The records of LFG flow and CH and O contents were electronically transferred to excel
4 2
spreadsheet and used to normalize the LFG flow to normal conditions (0 °C and 1.013 bar)
according and to calculate the hourly averages, and these averages are used to calculate the
volume of methane collected and methane emissions (flare efficiency) in the flare exhaust
gas. These figures are transferred to monthly excel spreadsheet and the emission reductions
(CERs) are calculated.
In addition, it was considered the “Net Flare Operational Period”, which corresponds to the
time during which the temperature of the flare is above 500ºC.
As the recordings of the field measurements are carried out automatically by the Landtec
Field service automatic unit which has several alarms to site operator and automatic actions,
(such as stop the operation of unit), the operation is considered safe, and the monitoring is

4 CERTIFICATION STATEMENT
DNV Climate Change Services AS (DNV) has performed the verification of the emission
reductions that have been reported for the CDM project activity 4211“Manaus Landfill Gas
Project” in Brazil for the period 1 February 2013 to 31 December 2013.
The project participants are responsible for the collection of data in accordance with the
monitoring plan and the reporting of GHG emissions reductions from the project activity.
It is DNV’s responsibility to express an independent verification statement on the reported
GHG emission reductions from the project activity. DNV does not express any opinion on the
selected baseline scenario or on the validated and registered PDD.
DNV conducted the verification on the basis of the baseline and monitoring methodology
ACM0001 (version 11), the monitoring plan contained in the PDD (version 4 of 24 May
2013) and the monitoring report (version 2) dated 7 April 2014. The verification included i)
checking whether the provisions of the monitoring methodology and the monitoring plan were
consistently and appropriately applied and ii) the collection of evidence supporting the
reported data.
DNV’s verification approach draws on an understanding of the risks associated with reporting
of GHG emission data and the controls in place to mitigate these. DNV planned and
performed the verification by obtaining evidence and other information and explanations that
DNV considers necessary to give reasonable assurance that reported GHG emission
reductions are fairly stated.
In our opinion the GHG emissions reductions reported for the project activity for the period 1
February 2013 to 31 December 2013 are fairly stated in the monitoring report (version 2)
dated 7 April 2014.
The GHG emission reductions were calculated correctly on the basis of the approved baseline
and monitoring methodology ACM0001 (version 11) and the monitoring plan contained in the
PDD (version 4 of 24 May 2013).
DNV Climate Change Services AS is able to certify that the emission reductions from the
CDM project activity 4211 “Manaus Landfill Gas Project” in Brazil during the period 1
February 2013 to 31 December 2013 amount to 374 294 tonnes of CO equivalent.
2

Felipe Lacerda Antunes
Felipe Antunes holds a Master’s Degree in Production Engineering (Quality) and a Post
Graduate Diploma in Environmental Management and Industrial Waste Management and
Treatment. Possesses an International experience of more than 10 years in the field of quality
and environmental auditing, working two years as the responsible of the QMS of Rede
Metrológica RS and since 1999 as a QMS and EMS auditor in DNV.
He has experience of more than 7 years in validation and verification of numerous CDM
projects in DNV, both in South America & abroad. He has also been actively involved in
Management System Audits such as ISO 9001, ISO 140001 and OHSAS 18001 standards in
various industrial sectors for more than 10 years in DNV.

energy generation from renewable energy sources, waste handling and disposal, and animal
waste management.

4. Outros Elementos
Campos de preenchimento opcional
4.1 Informações sobre a performance da organização, em comparação com benchmarks internos (ex: outras
unidades) ou externos (ex: organizações do mesmo setor).
Análise de benchmarks internos não são aplicáveis ao contexto da WayCarbon. Contudo, utiliza-se indicadores de
desempenho climático, que permitem o monitoramento mensal da performance da empresa neste tema.
Em relação a benchmarks externos, buscamos realizar análises comparativas utilizando como fontes de
informação os inventários mais recentes disponíveis no Registro Público do Programa Brasileiro do GHG Protocol,
publicados pelas empresas do mesmo setor da WayCarbon também participantes do Programa até 2014. Não foi
possível realizar análises comparativas de performance, seja pela inexistência de indicadores publicados ou pela
insuficiência de informações sobre a forma de cálculo dos índices divulgados.
4.2 Descrição de indicadores de emissão de GEE para as atividades da organização. Por exemplo, tCO e/produtos
2
fabricados.
Buscou-se construir um indicadores que representassem relação direta entre a atividade da empresa e suas
emissões. Para acompanhar o desempenho climático dos escritórios, utilizamos o indicador que consiste na soma
das emissões totais de GEE Kyoto (escopos 1, escopo 2 e escopo 3), divididas pelo total de homem-dia de trabalho.
Para o acompanhamento das emissões alocadas no nível da empresa, ou seja, para as emissões que não podem
ser atribuídas a um escritório específico, utiliza-se o indicador que consiste na soma das emissões totais de GEE
Kyoto (escopos 1, escopo 2 e escopo 3), divididas pelo faturamento total bruto.
4.3 Descrição de estratégias e projetos para a gestão de emissões de GEE.
O core business da WayCarbon refere-se à prestação de serviços de consultoria sobre gestão estratégica de
mudanças climáticas.
Sendo assim, um dos objetivos estratégicos da empresa consiste em empregar as melhores práticas de gestão de
emissões de GEE em seus processos internos. Nesse sentido, é nossa meta manter a qualificação “Ouro” de nosso
inventário no Programa Brasileiro do GHG Protocol. Além da gestão diligente, a WayCarbon compensa anualmente
100% de suas emissões de GEE de escopo 1, 2 e 3.

Summary:
DNV Climate Change Services AS (DNV) has performed the verification of the emission reductions
reported for the “Manaus Landfill Gas Project” (UNFCCC Registration Ref. No. 4211) for the period
8 July 2011 to 31 October 2011.
In our opinion, the GHG emission reductions reported for the project in the monitoring report
(version 3) of 30 May 2012 are fairly stated.
The GHG emission reductions were calculated correctly on the basis of the approved monitoring
methodology ACM0001 (version 11) and the monitoring plan contained in the Project Design
Document of 25 October 2010.
DNV Climate Change Services AS (DNV) is able to certify that the emission reductions from the
“Manaus Landfill Gas Project” during the period 8 July 2011 to 31 October 2011 amount to 53 283
tonnes of CO equivalent.

VERIFICATION / CERTIFICATION REPORT
1 INTRODUCTION
Tumpex Empresa Amazonense de Coleta de Lixo Ltda and Enterpa Engenharia Ltda from
Brazil have commissioned DNV Climate Change Services AS (DNV) to carry out the
verification and certification of emission reductions reported for the “Manaus Landfill Gas
Project” (the project) in the period 8 July 2011 to 31 October 2011. This report contains the
findings from the verification and a certification statement for the certified emission
reductions.
1.1 Objective
Verification is the periodic independent review and ex post determination by a Designated
Operational Entity (DOE) of the monitored reductions in GHG emissions that have occurred
as a result of the registered CDM project activity during a defined monitoring period.
Certification is the written assurance by a DOE that, during a specific period in time, a project
activity achieved the emission reductions as verified.
The objective of this verification was to verify and certify emission reductions reported for
the “Manaus Landfill Gas Project” for the period 8 July 2011 to 31 October 2011.
1.2 Scope
The scope of the verification is:
 To verify that actual monitoring systems and procedures are in compliance with
the monitoring systems and procedures described in the monitoring plan.
 To evaluate the GHG emission reduction data and express a conclusion with a
reasonable level of assurance about whether the reported GHG emission reduction
data is free from material misstatement.
 To verify that reported GHG emission data is sufficiently supported by evidence.
The verification shall ensure that reported emission reductions are complete and accurate in
order to be certified.
1.3 Description of the project activity
Project Parties: Brazil and Canada

The “Manaus Landfill Gas Project” was implemented with the objective to capture, flare and
generate electricity with the landfill gas generated at the landfill of Manaus, at Manaus
Municipality, in order to avoid emissions of methane to the atmosphere produced by the
anaerobic decomposition of the dumped waste from Manaus Municipality and to avoid
emissions of CO by the electricity supplied to the local grid.
2
The project involved the construction of a LFG collection system consisting of vertical and
horizontal LFG extraction wells, centrifugal blower(s), and all other supporting mechanical
and electrical subsystems and appurtenances necessary to collect the LFG. The facilities were
supplied by John Zink /34/ (enclosed flaring) and Landtec /35/ (capture and monitoring). The
project started operations on 8 July 2009. The LFG electricity generation component
contained in the registered PDD has not been installed yet. The PDD foresees electricity
generation start up in the second year of project operation. Since project registration, project
participant is seeking a potential electricity buyer in order to firm a long term power purchase
agreement and initiate the electricity generation procurement and construction.
1.4 Methodology for determining emission reductions
The verification of the emission reductions has assessed all factors and issues that constitute
the basis for emission reductions from the project. Based on the recommendations of
Executive Board’s “Validation and Verification Manual” 1.2,/26/ the DNV team has for this
assignment decided to check all factors and issues with the same emphasis.
DNV reviewed the project documentation; specifically the monitoring report for the period 8
July 2011 to 31 October 2011 and the registered PDD dated 25 October 2010 /31/.
The information provided in the monitoring report was assessed by:
- Verifying the compliance of actual monitoring at project site with the monitoring plan
included in the registered PDD /31/ as well as the monitoring methodology
ACM0001(version 11) /27/.
- Verifying the performance Landtec AEMS (Automated Extraction Monitoring
System) records /4/-/8/, assess the spreadsheet calculation /3/, calibration certificates
/11/-/15/, and hourly aggregation spreadsheet /9/. These relevant documents are
required for emission reduction calculations to assure that the data provided in the
monitoring report is reliable and that operation and maintenance of the monitoring
equipment is correct.
- Assessing of raw data from Landtec AEMS against the records provided by the project
participant by sampling. Since the record of each parameter is done every two
minutes, one day produces 720 records. Considering the monitoring period from 8
July 2011 to 31 October 2011, the total sample size is 83 520 line records. Sampling
procedure was based on the American Military Standard and tables for Inspection by
Attributes (MIL-STD-105E) /40/ Level II, single sample for normal inspection. Since

2.1 Review of documentation
The main documents assessed during the verification include:
i) Monitoring Report for the period 8 July 2011 to 31 October 2011, version 1 of 28
November 2011 /1/, which has been made publicly available on the CDM website on 5
December 2011 and the version 3 of 30 May 2012 /2/.
ii) The registered Project Design Document (PDD) dated 25 October 2010 /31/;
iii) The validation report, SGS Report CDM VAL 0377 2011 Rev 5, 29 June 2009 /33/.
Besides the above mentioned documents, DNV also assessed other additional documents that
were required to assess the accuracy of the emission reduction calculations presented in the
monitoring report /11/-/16/.
A list of key documents is given in the reference section of this report.
2.2 Site visit
From 20 December 2011 to 21 December 2011, Luis Filipe Tavares and Juliana Scalon
conducted an on-site audit at the “Manaus Landfill Gas Project” at Brazil. During this site
visit, DNV confirmed the actual implementation of the project against that mentioned in the
registered PDD /31/. This included confirming the operational stages of the project with
physical and documented evidence /4/-/8/. Figures quoted in the monitoring report were cross-
checked by reviewing operation records /9/ /10/. The persons interviewed during the audit are

listed in the reference section of this report /41/-/44/. Moreover, the supporting spreadsheets
for calculations of Manaus Landfill Gas Project CERs were checked during the visit /3/.
During this site visit and according to the presented document /31/, DNV was able to confirm
the landfill area, where the trenches and vertical wells were installed in order to collect the
LFG generated. The landfill is well operated, with waste compaction and daily cover with
soil, and the project’s capture and flaring station has the operation license /19/.
2.3 Reporting of findings
A corrective action request (CAR) is issued, where:
i. Non-conformities with the monitoring plan or methodology are found in monitoring
and reporting, or if the evidence provided to prove conformity is insufficient;
ii. Mistakes have been made in applying assumptions, data or calculations of emission
reductions which will impair the estimate of emission reductions;
iii. Issues identified in a FAR during validation to be verified during verification have not
been resolved by the project participants.
A clarification request (CL) shall be raised if information is insufficient or not clear enough to
determine whether the applicable CDM requirements have been met.
A forward action request (FAR) is issued for actions if the monitoring and reporting require
attention and/or adjustment for the next monitoring period.
During this verification, two CARs, six CLs and one FAR have been identified. One FAR was
identified in the validation related to the project environmental license. The CARs were
related to the application of the methane density in accordance to the normalized conditions of
the LFG flow, and also to the lack of thermocouples calibration. The CLs were all related to
further clarifications and/or corrections necessary to the Monitoring Report and the
calculation spreadsheet. These CARs, CLs and FAR were satisfactorily addressed by
Conestoga-Rovers & Associates Capital Limited by revising the monitoring report and
emission reduction calculations (refer to Appendix A).

3 VERIFICATION FINDINGS
This section summarises the findings from the verification of the emission reductions reported
for the “Manaus Landfill Gas Project” for the period 8 July 2011 to 31 October 2011.
3.1 Remaining issues, CARs, FARs from previous validation / verification
One FAR was identified during the validation. The project operation license should be
available during the first verification. Project participant has provided the operation license,
number 180/2010 /19/.
3.2 Project implementation
As part of the site visit DNV was able to confirm that the project implementation is in
accordance with the project description contained in registered PDD of 25 October 2010 /31/.
The LFG energy generation component foreseen in the registered PDD has not been installed
yet, since the PDD states that the electricity generation start-up will take place only during the
second year of project operations /31/. After the project registration, project participant is
seeking a potential electricity buyer in order to firm a long term power purchase agreement
and initiate the electricity generation procurement and construction during the year of 2012.
The landfill received waste in cells and after covering it was possible to install the wells or
trenches and piping to collect the LFG and conduct to the exhaust system.
After pumping, the LFG flow is measured through a unique thermal mass flow meter and
adjusted automatically 15ºC and 1 atm. In accordance with the methodology
ACM0001(version 11), no separate monitoring of temperature is necessary when using flow
meters that automatically measure temperature and pressure /11/.
The CH content of LFG is continuously sampled in the same section i.e. pipe section before
4
the flare, and measured automatically every 2 minutes /36/. A set of temperature meters have
been installed at 4 places on the flare and control the inlet dampers to control optimum flare
conditions, and ensure that the temperature inside the flare remains above 500ºC /13/.
The CH and O content of the flare exhaust gas are continuously sampled in the flare outlet,
4 2
and measured automatically every 2 minutes /15/.
All monitoring is carried out through Landtec AEMS (Automated Extraction Monitoring
System) /35/ which collects all monitoring and control parameters and automatically sends the
measurement results through internet to Landtec server in USA including any operation
alarms and troubleshooting. The system has local back-up for 24 hours and continuous back-
up at the Landtec server /35/.
3.3 Information (data and variables) provided in the monitoring report
that is different from that stated in the registered PDD
The emission reductions are based on the figures sourced from the Landtec AEMS records.
During the monitoring period 8 July 2011 to 31 October 2011 information provided in the
monitoring report /1/ was compared to the data and variables stated in the registered PDD /31/
with regard to:
- Flow of LFG to the flare: The parameter is estimated ex-ante in the PDD based on the
potential of LFG generation in the landfilled waste and the capture efficiency of the
Page 7
DET NORSKE VERITAS
Report No: 2011-1714, rev. 02
VERIFICATION / CERTIFICATION REPORT
LFG in the waste mass. In the CER calculation spreadsheet of the registered PDD /32/,
the estimated average amount for the year 2011 was 87 509 695 Nm3 (or 35 003 878
Nm3 for four months). The amount of LFG captured during monitoring period of 8
July 2011 to 31 October 2011 was 7 853 907 Nm3 /3/, i.e., 77.56% less than estimated.
The difference is related to restriction in the LFG capture system. During the site visit
/41/-/44/, DNV could confirm that the capture system in the landfill was not entirely
implemented, and the drainage trenches in the waste mass were under construction.
Hence, there are improvements in the collection system to be done. The project
participant is improving the quality and quantity of wells, trenches and pipelines in
order to reach the expected amount of captured LFG declared in the PDD;
- Methane content in the LFG collected and sent to the flare. In order to estimate the
CERs in the PDD, the methane content in the LFG was admitted as 50%. However,
during the monitoring period of 8 July 2011 to 31 October 2011, the average methane
content was about 45% /3/. This methane content depends also on the quality and
quantity of wells, trenches and pipelines installed and the improvements in the
management of the whole LFG capturing system.
- Temperature of the flare: no differences in the flare temperature was detected. The
flare is subject to regular maintenance in order to maintain favourable burning
conditions. The methane destruction efficiency is directly related to the flare
temperature. In order to estimate the CERs in the registered PDD /32/, the flare
efficiency was considered 98%. During the monitoring period of 8 July 2011 to 31
October 2011, the flare efficiency remained above 99%.
3.4 Compliance of monitoring plan with monitoring methodology
DNV is able to confirm that the project is in accordance with the approved methodology
applied by the project activity, i.e. ACM0001 (version 11).
3.5 Compliance of monitoring with the monitoring plan
The monitoring has been carried out in accordance with the monitoring methodology
ACM0001(version 11) that requires the monitoring of the:
- Total amount of landfill gas captured and flared through a thermo mass flow meter
/11/. This flow meter provides the volume in imperial standard cubic meters (15°C and
1 atm);
- The methane fraction in the landfill gas, verified by the gas analyser FAU;
- The methane concentration (ppm) in the flare exhaust gas, measured by the flare gas
analyser FEA,
- The oxygen fraction (%) in the flare exhaust gas, measured by the flare gas analyser
FEA;
- The flare temperature (°C) and,
- The total amount of electricity imported from the grid to meet project requirements,
measured by the local power distribution company that supplies the project /18/.
In addition, the CO emission intensity of electricity imported from the Brazilian Northern
2
Isolated System is considered according to the factor calculated ex-ante by the project
participant /20/, the transmissions and distribution losses was updated /39/.

3.6 Assessment of data and calculation of emission reductions
The emission reduction calculations are based on the figures sourced from the Landtec AEMS
historical records for the following parameters:
- Flow of LFG to the flare;
- Methane content in the LFG collected and sent to the flare;
- Temperature of the flare;
- Methane and oxygen content in the flare exhaust gases.
These data are transferred into a master excel spreadsheet used to aggregate the hourly values
of the recorded 2 minutes figures according to the methodology. These hourly numbers are
transferred to monthly spreadsheet calculations which determine the volume of LFG, and the
CO equivalent reduced (tCO e), whilst considering the methane density of 0.0007168
2 2
(tonCH /m3CH ) for (0°C and 1atm) and GWP of 21 as established on ACM0001, version 11.
4 4
Page 34
DET NORSKE VERITAS
Report No: 2011-1714, rev. 02
VERIFICATION / CERTIFICATION REPORT
The methane density of 0.0007168 tCH /m3CH stated in the PDD /31/ corresponds to the
4 4
International Standard Temperature and Pressure (0°C and 1 atm) /38/ while the thermal mass
flow meter used in the “Manaus Landfill Gas Project” gives the LFG flow on Imperial
Standard Temperature and Pressure (15°C and 1atm). The monitoring plan contained in the
registered PDD states that “for reporting purposes, the flows are generally required to be
normalized to 0°C and 1.013 bar (1atm)”. Hence, the LFG flow was adjusted to 0°C in the
master hourly spreadsheets /9/.
The electricity consumed is measured by an electricity meter at the project site, installed by
the local electricity distribution company (Amazonas Energia) /17/.
The emission reductions for the period have been analysed against the estimated CERs in the
PDD /31/ and the actual verified emission reductions are lower than the estimated emission
reductions as stated in the table below.

Amount of CER variations are related to LFG capture conditions that were different than the
expected by the project participant during the PDD /31/ development. The difference from
CERs estimated in the PDD is related to restriction in the LFG capture system. The present
verification corresponds to the first four months of operation of the project activity. Hence,
there are improvements in the collection system to be done. The project participant is
improving the quality and quantity of wells and pipeline in order to reach the expected
amount of captured LFG declared in the PDD.
3.7 Quality of evidence to determine emission reductions
DNV was able to confirm that the calculations are based on the authentic data from the
Landtec AEMS historical records /4/-/8/ and also the monthly systematic and periodical
monitoring of the electricity consumption by the project /18/. The spreadsheets used to
aggregate the hourly values of the 2 minutes recording figures and the spreadsheets /9/ used to
calculate the monthly certified emission reductions (CER) calculations /3/ and all figures were
tracked, checked by sampling /10/ and found to be consistent.
The monitoring information is stored on server computer of Landtec site, and the spreadsheets
are stored on server of Conestoga-Rovers Waterloo/Ontario, both with regular and systematic
back-up.
3.8 Management system and quality assurance
The monitoring is carried out through the Landtec Field Service automatic unit /36/, which
include beyond several other parameters, the follow as the main:
LFG flow adjusted to Imperial Standard conditions (15 ºC and 1.013 bar);

CH content of LFG, including the calibration with certified standard gas;
 4
CH and O content in the exhaust gas, including the daily calibration with certified
 4 2
standard gas, and
Page 35
DET NORSKE VERITAS
Report No: 2011-1714, rev. 02
VERIFICATION / CERTIFICATION REPORT
Flare temperature on three levels, which is considered to calculate the “Net Flare

Operational Period”.
These figures are registered every two minutes and stored in Landtec recorder system at
Landtec server, connected through internet with controlled password. In addition, these are
stored as PDF copies on local computer and safety copies on CDR media, stored on the
Manaus Landfill Gas Project sites.
The records of LFG flow and CH and O contents were electronically transferred to excel
4 2
spreadsheet and used to normalize the LFG flow to normal conditions (0 °C and 1.013 bar)
according and to calculate the hourly averages, and these averages are used to calculate the
volume of methane collected and methane emissions (flare efficiency) in the flare exhaust
gas. These figures are transferred to monthly excel spreadsheet and the emission reductions
(CERs) are calculated.
In addition, it was considered the “Net Flare Operational Period”, which corresponds to the
time during which the temperature of the flare is above 500ºC. There was a period when the
thermocouples were not covered by a calibration. Project participant took into consideration
the guidelines for assessing compliance with the calibration frequency requirements /30/ and
applied maximum permissible error from the calibration (1%) /13/ to the minimum accepted
flare temperature (500°C). This temperature is the minimum accepted so the recorded data
(every two minutes) can be included in the emission reductions calculations.
The corrected temperature applied resulted in 32 minutes of invalid data (temperatures
between 501 °C and 505 °C), decreasing the overall emission reductions of the monitoring
period. All calculation is carried out at Conestoga-Rovers Waterloo/Ontario office which is
registered under the ISO 9001:2000 standard.
As the recordings of the field measurements are carried out automatically by the Landtec
Field service automatic unit which has several alarms to site operator and automatic actions,
(such as stop the operation of unit), the operation is considered safe, and the monitoring is
considered reliable. In addition, as verified, the electronic handling of records, the calculation,
the monitoring and reporting are deemed to be consistent.
Moreover, preventive maintenance is developed on all instrumentation, assuring adequate
calibration. The electronic information is stored on Landtec AEMS /35/ as well as on monthly
CDs /4/-/8/.

4 CERTIFICATION STATEMENT
DNV Climate Change Services AS (DNV) has performed the verification of the emission
reductions that have been reported for the “Manaus Landfill Gas Project” (UNFCCC
Registration Reference No. 4211), for the period 8 July 2011 to 31 October 2011.
The project participants are responsible for the collection of data in accordance with the
registered PDD of 25 October 2010 and the reporting of GHG emissions reductions from the
project.
It is DNV’s responsibility to express an independent verification statement on the reported
GHG emission reductions from the project. DNV does not express any opinion on the
selected baseline scenario or on the validated and registered PDD /31/.
DNV conducted the verification on the basis of the monitoring methodology ACM0001
version 11 and the monitoring plan contained in the registered Project Design Document of 25
October 2010. The verification included i) checking whether the provisions of the monitoring
methodology and the monitoring plan were consistently and appropriately applied and ii) the
collection of evidence supporting the reported data.
DNV’s verification approach draws on an understanding of the risks associated with reporting
of GHG emission data and the controls in place to mitigate these. DNV planned and
performed the verification by obtaining evidence and other information and explanations that
DNV considers necessary to give reasonable assurance that reported GHG emission
reductions are fairly stated.
In our opinion, the GHG emissions reductions of the “Manaus Landfill Gas Project”
(UNFCCC Registration Ref. No. 4211) for the period 8 July 2011 to 31 October 2011 are
fairly stated in the monitoring report version 3 dated 30 May 2012.
The GHG emission reductions were calculated correctly on the basis of the approved baseline
and monitoring methodology ACM0001 version 11 and the registered PDD of 25 October
2010 /31/.
DNV Climate Change Services AS is able to certify that the emission reductions from the
“Manaus Landfill Gas Project” during the period 8 July 2011 to 31 October 2011 amount to
53 283 tonnes of CO equivalent.

PPENDIX
CURRICULA VITAE OF THE VERIFICATION TEAM MEMBERS
DET NORSKE VERITAS
Luis Filipe Tavares
Mr. Luis Filipe Tavares holds a Technician’s Degree in Chemistry and Bachelor’s Degree in
Metallurgical Engineering, having an overall experience of thirty tree years.
Prior to joining DNV having around twenty tree years’ experience in steel production industry
covering utilities (water, steam, wastewater treatment), environment control (atmosphere
emissions, water emission and waste dumping).
His experience also covers the development of nitrification biological wastewater station as
well as other activities as head of Utilities and Environmental Laboratory control.
He has also been actively involved in implementation of Management Systems such as ISO
9001 standard on coke oven department of steel industry as well as the ISO 140001 standard
in all steel plant (the second steel company certified in the world) for more than three years.
He start on DNV as ISO 9001, ISO 14001 and OHSAS lead auditor, certifying numerous
management systems during 7 years.
He has experience of around 8 years in validation and verification of numerous CDM projects
in DNV, both in Brazil & South America.
His qualification, industrial experience and experience in CDM demonstrate his sufficient
sectoral competence in Iron and Steel; Metal production; Oil and Gas industry, CMM
recovery and use; Generation from renewable energy sources; Waste handling and disposal
and Animal waste management.
Juliana Scalon
Ms. Juliana Scalon holds a Bachelor Degree in Civil Engineering having an overall
experience of around 11 years. Prior to joining DNV having 5.5 years of experience in waste
handling and disposal service industry, covering technical operation and environment aspects
of landfills and gas management, and 5 years of experience in CDM consultancy services,
responsible for the development of several Project Design Documents for landfill gas projects,
project management on CDM projects of renewables, transport, and the development of
greenhouse gas inventories for chemical industry.
She acts in the team for validation and verification of CDM projects/JI and other 3rd party
validation/verification services.
Her qualification, industrial experience and experience in CDM demonstrate her sufficient
sectoral competence in waste handling and disposal.
Kumaraswamy Chandrashekara
DET NORSKE VERITAS
Kumaraswamy Chandrashekara holds a Bachelor’s Degree in Chemical Engineering and has
an overall experience of around 24 years. Prior to joining DNV, has worked for 11 years in
the Chemical Process Industry covering Plant Operations, Technical Services and Process
Design activities, primarily in the fertilisers and chemicals manufacturing sector. During this
tenure of 11 years in the industry, responsibilities included production, process optimization,
energy efficiency improvements, environmental performance, process design, energy auditing
and technical auditing.
He has experience of around six years in the validation and verification of numerous CDM
projects both in India and abroad. His qualification, industrial experience and experience in
CDM sufficiently demonstrate his sectoral competence in the areas of chemical process
industries, energy generation from renewable sources and waste handling & disposal
Ravi Kumar Prabhu
Mr. Ravi Kumar Prabhu holds Bachelor’s Degree in Chemical Engineering and has done Post
Graduate Diploma course in Management and has an overall working experience of around
twenty six years. Prior to joining DNV has around twenty three years experience in Chemical
process industry (fertilizer & petrochemical manufacturing) covering production, technical
services including energy audits and efficiency studies, waste heat recovery, efficiency studies
of boilers, power plants, safety audits, pollution control activities and waste water treatment.
With respect to the Thermal Power Plant, the job assignment included the monitoring of flue
gas stack temperatures and excess air, efficacy of fuel additives, condition of boiler refractory
and insulation of steam lines, residual life assessment of boilers etc. His experience also
includes 7 years in the Process design of fertilizer & petrochemical plants, wherein he was
involved in the development of process flow diagrams, development of P&IDs, equipment
design, HAZOP studies, procurement and commissioning activities.
He has over three years experience in validation and verification of CDM projects in DNV
and is also an EMS lead auditor.
His qualification, industrial experience and experience in CDM projects demonstrate
sufficient sectoral competence in Chemical Process Industries (TA 5.1), Thermal Energy
Generation from fossil fuels (TA1.1), Heat distribution (TA 2.2), Energy generation from
Renewable Energy sources (TA 1.2) and Waste handling and disposal (TA 13.1).

Informações institucionais
A Whirlpool, dona das marcas Brastemp, Consul e KitchenAid, é líder do mercado latino-
americano de eletrodomésticos. É reconhecida e premiada por oferecer produtos
inovadores aos consumidores, desenvolver e atrair talentos de alto desempenho e por ser
comprometida com a sustentabilidade. Admirada por suas marcas, a empresa leva a
qualidade, preocupação com o meio ambiente e paixão por seus produtos para cerca de
100 milhões de lares brasileiros, o que representa um produto a cada dois domicílios.
Atualmente, conta com cerca de 11 mil funcionários distribuídos entre o centro
administrativo e as fábricas localizadas em Joinville (Santa Catarina), Manaus (Amazonas)
e Rio Claro (São Paulo), além de 23 laboratórios de Pesquisa e Desenvolvimento e quatro
Centros de Tecnologia. Na América Latina, tem escritórios na Argentina, Chile, Peru,
Guatemala, Equador, Colômbia, Porto Rico, República Dominicana e Miami.

4. Outros Elementos
Campos de preenchimento opcional
4.1 Informações sobre a performance da organização, em comparação com benchmarks internos (ex: outras
unidades) ou externos (ex: organizações do mesmo setor).
Não realizado.
4.2 Descrição de indicadores de emissão de GEE para as atividades da organização. Por exemplo, tCO e/produtos
2
fabricados.
Temos os indicadores de Escopo 1 em g CO2eq / R$ de faturamento bruto e também o indicador de Escopo 1 em kg
de CO2eq / produto fabricado.
Utilizamos para acompanhamento interno dos valores de emissão por Escopo. A gestão é realizada para os
indicadores que temos ação direta, que são o consumo de combustíveis fósseis, o consumo de energia elétrica, que
são os programas de aumento de eficiência que temos nas operações, procurando reduzir desperdícios e
otimizando o uso e operação dos processos internos da empresa. Dessa forma acompanhamos somente
internamente os indicadores de emissão Escopo 1 (principalmente) relativos ao faturamento e relativos ao volume
de produtos fabricados, para ver como se comportam frente às nossas ações internas de otimização das operações.
4.3 Descrição de estratégias e projetos para a gestão de emissões de GEE.
Estão atrelados aos programas de redução do consumo de energia elétrica e ao programa de redução do consumo
de combustíveis fósseis. O que não conseguimos controlar são as variações do fator de conversão escopo 2 -
energia elétrica. Outro fator de difícil controle é a intensidade de distribuição dos produtos no primeiro trecho (que
conseguimos monitorar) e que é resultado de forte otimização das rotas de distribuição logística mas fortemente
influenciado pela quantidade de produtos fabricados e vendidos no ano.
4.4 Informações sobre contratos com clientes e fornecedores que incluam cláusulas vinculadas à elaboração de
inventários de GEE e/ou ao envio de informações relacionadas.
Não há.
4.5 Informações sobre incertezas, exclusões de fontes de dados e outras características da elaboração do
inventário.
Não temos em geral incertezas pois nossos dados são baseados em valores reais consumidos ou utilizados.
4.6 Descrição sobre ações internas para melhoria da qualidade do inventário de GEE. Por exemplo, sistematização
da coleta de dados, contratação de verificação externa, etc.
Não há.
4.7 Informações sobre a compra de energia elétrica oriunda de fonte renovável.
Não foi relatado.

Informações institucionais
A Whirlpool, dona das marcas Brastemp, Consul e KitchenAid, é líder do mercado latino-
americano de eletrodomésticos. É reconhecida e premiada por oferecer produtos
inovadores aos consumidores, desenvolver e atrair talentos de alto desempenho e por ser
comprometida com a sustentabilidade. Admirada por suas marcas, a empresa leva a
qualidade, preocupação com o meio ambiente e paixão por seus produtos para cerca de
100 milhões de lares brasileiros, o que representa um produto a cada dois domicílios.
Atualmente, conta com cerca de 11 mil funcionários distribuídos entre o centro
administrativo e as fábricas localizadas em Joinville (Santa Catarina), Manaus (Amazonas)
e Rio Claro (São Paulo), além de 23 laboratórios de Pesquisa e Desenvolvimento e quatro
Centros de Tecnologia. Na América Latina, tem escritórios na Argentina, Chile, Peru,
Guatemala, Equador, Colômbia, Porto Rico, República Dominicana e Miami.

Fontes de emissão não reportadas
Não foi possível contabilizar as emissões do HFC 134a, utilizado nos processos de fabricação de refrigeradores e
freezers, como gás do circuito de refrigeração. Essa é uma emissão fugitiva e como produzimos centenas de
modelos diferentes por mês e um volume de produção de aproximadamente 250.000 produtos por mês, não temos
método desenvolvido para essa contabilização.
Também não conseguimos gerar dados confiáveis para medir as emissões das frotas de veículos próprias, que
devem ser desprezíveis pela pequena quantidade de veículos próprios. E também não conseguimos dados
confiáveis sobre as emissões dos terceiros (transportadoras) que transportam nossos produtos no Brasil.

4.3 Descrição de estratégias e projetos para a gestão de emissões de GEE.
Ao longo dos últimos anos estabelecemos um indicador de consumo de energia por produto fabricado com base
mensal. Estabelecemos metas de melhoria contínua que foram atingidas através de campanhas de redução de
desperdício, de substituição de motores tradicionais por motores de alto rendimento, de substituição de processos
antigos por mais modernos e eficientes, etc.
Outra ação importante se refere à substituição de combustíveis, como, por exemplo, a eliminação do consumo de
óleo combustível e sua substituição pelo uso de gás natural.

da coleta de dados, contratação de verificação externa, etc.
Estamos elaborando uma planilha de controle mensal para a coleta e registro dos dados de 2009.
Estamos buscando ampliar para 2009 a coleta de dados de combustíveis gastos na frota de carros usadas
internamente pela empresa, apesar de prevermos que suas emissões serão pequenas e desprezíveis se
comparadas com as outras fontes. Também estamos procurando contabilizar o consumo de combustíveis na
logística de distribuição de nossos produtos no Brasil, atividade que é terceirizada com empresas de transporte.

Informações institucionais
A Whirlpool, dona das marcas Brastemp, Consul e KitchenAid, é líder do mercado latino-
americano de eletrodomésticos. É reconhecida e premiada por oferecer produtos
inovadores aos consumidores, desenvolver e atrair talentos de alto desempenho e por ser
comprometida com a sustentabilidade. Admirada por suas marcas, a empresa leva a
qualidade, preocupação com o meio ambiente e paixão por seus produtos para cerca de
100 milhões de lares brasileiros, o que representa um produto a cada dois domicílios.
Atualmente, conta com cerca de 11 mil funcionários distribuídos entre o centro
administrativo e as fábricas localizadas em Joinville (Santa Catarina), Manaus (Amazonas)
e Rio Claro (São Paulo), além de 23 laboratórios de Pesquisa e Desenvolvimento e quatro
Centros de Tecnologia. Na América Latina, tem escritórios na Argentina, Chile, Peru,
Guatemala, Equador, Colômbia, Porto Rico, República Dominicana e Miami.

Inventário 2011 - Whirlpool
4. Outros Elementos
Campos de preenchimento opcional
4.1 Informações sobre a performance da organização, em comparação com benchmarks internos (ex: outras
unidades) ou externos (ex: organizações do mesmo setor).
Consideramos que não somos uma empresa de forte intensidade carbônica, temos mais uma característica de
montadora e não emitimos significativamente. Comparativamente, nossos produtos na fase de uso na casa dos
consumidores tem uma emissão de CO2 de 80% comparado aos valores do inventário (20%) em uma mesma base
anual.
4.2 Descrição de indicadores de emissão de GEE para as atividades da organização. Por exemplo, tCO e/produtos
2
fabricados.
avaliamos os valores absolutos.
4.3 Descrição de estratégias e projetos para a gestão de emissões de GEE.
Dentro dos Pilares de Sustentabilidade as emissões de CO2 tem duas ações principais: 1) melhorar a eficiência
energética dos produtos, com cada vez menor consumo de energia e 2) melhoria contínua dos indicadores fabris de
consumo de energia por produto fabricado e consumo de combustíveis fósseis. Na Logística temos um programa
de otimização na distribuição de produtos, envolvendo tanto melhor carregamento dos caminhões quanto
otimizações dos roteiros de distribuição.
4.4 Informações sobre contratos com clientes e fornecedores que incluam cláusulas vinculadas à elaboração de
inventários de GEE e/ou ao envio de informações relacionadas.
Não temos.
4.5 Informações sobre incertezas, exclusões de fontes de dados e outras características da elaboração do
inventário.
Somente parte dos dados de emissões de fontes móveis que se referem aos veículos dos gestores, que não temos
100% de certeza na informação, sobre se abastecem com álcool ou gasolina, utilizo a pior hipótese, de que é
sempre abastecido com gasolina, e mesmo assim isso representa 1,3% do total de emissões. Outro ponto é o item
novo na ferramenta, de emissões por disposição de resíduos em aterros sanitários, conseguimos contabilizar este
ano somente para o site mais significativo (Joinville), que representou somente 0,06% do inventário. Os outros sites
juntos não chegam nos valores de Joinville.
4.6 Descrição sobre ações internas para melhoria da qualidade do inventário de GEE. Por exemplo, sistematização
da coleta de dados, contratação de verificação externa, etc.
Ainda não decidimos por auditar o inventário, estamos mantendo a política de qualidade do inventário que tivemos
no ano passado com a obtenção do selo prata.
4.7 Informações sobre a compra de energia elétrica oriunda de fonte renovável.
